key_claim: Training on model-generated content causes irreversible model collapse where tails of the original content distribution disappear across generations of learned generative models.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of Gaussian and discrete distributions plus empirical experiments fine-tuning GPT-2 on wikitext2 with iterative retraining on generated data across multiple generations
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether incorporating physically-verified authentic human behavioral data could serve as a corrective signal to prevent tail collapse in iterative training.
theoretical_framework: Model collapse framework distinguishing early collapse (tail disappearance) and late collapse (distribution convergence to low-variance state), decomposing error into statistical sampling error and functional approximation error

key_claim: Diffusion models can self-improve using their own synthetic data without model collapse by using synthetic data as negative guidance rather than aggregating it with real data.
methodology: Training diffusion models (EDM-VP) with SIMS algorithm that uses synthetic data for negative guidance during generation, evaluated on CIFAR-10 and FFHQ-64 with FID metrics
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether the self-improvement approach can recover lost tail distribution information once real data diversity has already declined due to web-scraped data pollution.
theoretical_framework: Self-IMproving diffusion models with Synthetic data (SIMS) — negative guidance framework treating synthetic data as a signal to steer away from non-ideal distributions

key_claim: Introducing a correction function that maps data points to be more likely under the true distribution makes self-consuming generative training loops exponentially more stable.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of idealized correction functions for self-consuming loops plus empirical validation using self-correction functions based on expert knowledge (e.g., physics simulators)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how to construct correction functions when the true data distribution is unknown or when authentic human behavioral signals—rather than physics simulators—are needed as ground truth.
theoretical_framework: Idealized correction function framework for stabilizing self-consuming generative model training loops

key_claim: Indiscriminate use of model-generated content in training causes irreversible model collapse where tails of the original distribution disappear due to compounding statistical and functional approximation errors.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of discrete distributions and Gaussians decomposing model collapse into statistical sampling error and functional expressivity error, plus empirical validation fine-tuning GPT-2 on wikitext2 across multiple generations with five-way beam search
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether curating training data from verified authentic human social behavior sources could prevent or mitigate the tail collapse and diversity loss demonstrated in recursive training.
theoretical_framework: Model collapse framework with formal decomposition into early collapse (tail disappearance) and late collapse (distribution convergence), driven by statistical error and functional expressivity error

key_claim: Data curation in self-consuming generative model loops provably increases expected reward and collapses reward variance, establishing close connections with RLHF and preference optimization.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of iterative retraining with K-choice curation, deriving convergence results (Theorem 2.1) and stability guarantees (Theorems 2.3, 2.4) with supporting experiments on synthetic distributions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether curated synthetic data loops lead to the loss of rare or tail-distribution information that authentic human behavioral data uniquely provides.
theoretical_framework: Iterative discrete K-choice curation framework with best-of-K tournament selection, connected to RLHF via reward-weighted density updates

key_claim: Neon improves generative models by extrapolating away from the degradation direction caused by self-training, redistributing probability mass from over-represented to under-represented modes.
methodology: Negative extrapolation in parameter space: fine-tune a base model on its own synthetic outputs to identify the degradation direction, then move in the opposite direction; evaluated across diffusion, flow matching, autoregressive, and few-step generators.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the recovered diversity through negative extrapolation can restore tail-distribution signals lost from web-scraped data contaminated by synthetic content.
theoretical_framework: Anti-alignment analysis between synthetic and population data gradients under mode-seeking inference samplers

key_claim: Surplexity, a measure of model surprise, can detect and mitigate model collapse by filtering training data without requiring knowledge of whether data is human-authored or AI-generated.
methodology: Introduce surplexity metric to characterize collapsed models from their probability distributions; use it to filter training documents during autophagy simulations across multiple LLMs and datasets.
limitation: Current experiments rely on simulations rather than large-scale longitudinal empirical studies, and the approach has not been validated at the scale of production LLM training.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not assess whether surplexity-based filtering can preserve the unique informational signals present in authentic human social behavioral data versus merely maintaining distributional diversity.
theoretical_framework: Surplexity framework for measuring model surprise as a proxy for distributional collapse

key_claim: LLMs trained in a self-consuming loop can initially improve output correctness but inevitably suffer drastic diversity loss that collapses outputs to a single point.
methodology: Iterative retraining of transformer language models from scratch on self-generated logic expression data across multiple data cycles, measuring correctness and diversity over generations.
limitation: The study uses a synthetic logic expression dataset rather than natural language, limiting direct generalizability to real-world LLM training scenarios.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how incorporating physically-verified authentic human behavioral data, as opposed to generic fresh data, could differentially mitigate diversity collapse in the self-consuming loop.

key_claim: Preventing model collapse in self-consuming training loops requires maintaining recursive stability and a sufficient proportion of real data relative to model architecture complexity.
methodology: Introduced recursive stability concept and derived generalization error bounds for self-consuming training loops, analyzing transformer architectures on mixed real-synthetic datasets.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how the quality or diversity characteristics of the original real data (e.g., web-scraped vs. physically-verified behavioral data) affect recursive stability thresholds.
theoretical_framework: Recursive stability framework with generalization error bounds for self-consuming training loops

key_claim: Model collapse is unavoidable when training solely on synthetic data, as the tails of the original distribution disappear, but careful control of synthetic-to-real data ratio can limit distributional deviation.
methodology: Statistical analysis using a Markov chain model on the probability simplex to characterize recursive training scenarios for language models with multinomial distributions.
limitation: Results describe only the statistical approximation error since all generation models are unbiased; does not account for model misspecification or optimization bias.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether certain types of real data (e.g., authentic human behavioral data) provide stronger anchoring signals against tail collapse than generic web-scraped data.
theoretical_framework: Markov chain model on probability simplex with absorbing states for recursive synthetic data generation

key_claim: Latent space filtering of synthetic training data based on degradation of low-dimensional latent representations can stabilize self-consuming diffusion model loops without requiring fresh real data.
methodology: Empirical analysis of latent space dynamics in self-consuming DDPM diffusion models, proposing Latent Space Filtering (LSF) to remove unrealistic synthetic images based on representation misalignment across generations.
limitation: The approach relies on latent representations extracted by diffusion models which themselves may degrade; the filtering mechanism's effectiveness is demonstrated only on diffusion models for image synthesis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether filtering approaches can compensate for the fundamental loss of tail diversity in training data or whether authentic human-generated data provides irreplaceable distributional signals.

key_claim: Spectral analysis of embedding Gram matrices can scalably detect and predict model collapse onset, revealing that weight carryover accelerates geometric contraction of representational diversity beyond data-only recursion.
methodology: Derived deterministic and stochastic spectral bounds (Weyl's inequality, Ky Fan dominance) on Gram matrices of LLM embeddings, with empirical validation on recursive training loops comparing data-only vs. weight-carryover scenarios.
limitation: The deterministic bound depends on the spectral radius of the unobserved data component (β_k), making it loose in practice; scalable estimators rely on sub-sampling assumptions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the spectral health metrics differ when training on authentic human behavioral data versus web-scraped synthetic-contaminated corpora, leaving open whether data provenance affects collapse trajectories.
theoretical_framework: SIGMA (Spectral Inequalities for Gram Matrix Analysis)

key_claim: Discrete distribution estimation in self-consuming loops does not necessarily collapse if the mixing factor of real data remains constant or decays sufficiently slowly.
methodology: Minimax analysis deriving upper and lower bounds on expected loss for recursive discrete distribution estimation under self-consuming loops with mixed real and synthetic samples.
limitation: Results do not directly apply to practical settings because lower bounds require the effective number of samples to be comparable to the alphabet size, which may be very large in practice.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether real-world web-scraped corpora exhibit the mixing conditions (sufficient fresh authentic data) needed to prevent collapse in practice.
theoretical_framework: Minimax discrete distribution estimation framework for self-consuming generative loops with parameterized real/synthetic mixing factors

key_claim: Co-evolving text-image generative models accelerate mutual collapse via a Matthew effect that reinforces dominant texts, but external data injections can stabilize the system.
methodology: Analytical modeling of co-evolving text-image systems with text as multinomial distribution and images as Gaussian mixtures, studying convergence dynamics and stabilization through corpus and user-content injections.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether authentic human behavioral data specifically (as opposed to generic fresh data) provides qualitatively different stabilization signals for co-evolving models.
theoretical_framework: Co-evolving generative model framework with multinomial text and Gaussian mixture image models under iterative feedback

key_claim: Model collapse in diffusion models manifests as a generalization-to-memorization transition driven by declining entropy of training data across recursive iterations.
methodology: Empirical analysis of diffusion models under recursive self-consuming training, measuring entropy decline, nearest-neighbor distances, and FID; proposed entropy-based data selection strategy to mitigate collapse.
limitation: The paper does not rigorously explain why the entropy of generated data decreases over iterations; the theoretical mechanism underlying the transition is left for future work.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether physically-verified authentic human data provides fundamentally different entropy characteristics compared to high-quality synthetic data for mitigating collapse.
theoretical_framework: Entropy-based characterization of model collapse as generalization-to-memorization transition

key_claim: Without enough fresh real data in each generation, self-consuming generative models inevitably suffer Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD), losing either quality or diversity.
methodology: Analytical and empirical analysis of three families of autophagous loops (fully synthetic, synthetic augmentation, fresh data) using conditional DDPMs on MNIST and StyleGAN2 with varying sampling bias parameters.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not quantify how much or what type of fresh real data (e.g., physically-verified human behavioral data vs. generic web-scraped data) is needed to prevent MAD in practice.
theoretical_framework: Autophagous loop taxonomy (fully synthetic, synthetic augmentation, fresh data loops) with sampling bias analysis

key_claim: Without sufficient fresh real data in each generation, self-consuming generative models inevitably suffer Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD), losing either quality or diversity.
methodology: Analytical and empirical analysis of three autophagous loop families using conditional DDPMs on MNIST and state-of-the-art image generative models with varying sampling bias parameters
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not quantify how the progressive contamination of web-scraped corpora with synthetic content specifically degrades tail-distribution representations of authentic human behavioral data.
theoretical_framework: Autophagous loop taxonomy (fully synthetic loop, synthetic augmentation loop, fresh data loop) with sampling bias parameter λ

key_claim: Increasing the number of distinct language models in an AI ecosystem and segmenting training data across them mitigates model collapse over iterative self-training.
methodology: Segmenting training data across ecosystems of 1, 2, 4, and 16 identical pre-trained language models fine-tuned on non-overlapping subsets, evaluated over 10 self-training iterations using mean perplexity
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether epistemic diversity can recover lost tail-distribution knowledge or preserve authentic human social behavioral signals already absent from training data.
theoretical_framework: Ecology-inspired AI ecosystem diversity framework with optimal diversity level monotonically increasing with self-training iterations

key_claim: GenAI follows a 'democratization–average trap–model collapse' trajectory where next-token prediction produces generic outputs lacking individuality and recursive training amplifies machine bias.
methodology: Conceptual analysis projecting a novel GenAI trajectory framework applied to consumer research challenges
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically measure the extent of information degradation in web-scraped consumer behavior data or compare it against physically-verified authentic behavioral signals.

key_claim: Augmenting real data with synthetic data across generations universally preserves a fraction π²/6 of statistical efficiency, avoiding model collapse regardless of the specific model family.
methodology: Mathematical proof via contiguity arguments in exponential family models, validated with logistic regression experiments on UCI tabular datasets comparing discard vs. augment workflows
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether the π²/6 efficiency guarantee holds when the original real data itself suffers from diversity loss or tail collapse due to web-scraping biases.
theoretical_framework: Universal π²/6 statistical efficiency pathway for augment workflows in exponential family generative models

key_claim: LLM output homogenization is a fundamentally underresearched phenomenon closely related to model collapse and algorithmic biases, constituting a form of 'how-bullshit'.
methodology: Conceptual analysis linking output homogenization to model collapse and algorithmic bias literature, illustrated with visual and textual examples from ChatGPT outputs
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically measure the degree of output homogenization or quantify diversity loss in LLM-generated text relative to authentic human data distributions.
theoretical_framework: Reconceptualization of LLM output homogenization as 'how-bullshit' (how-BS), linking it to model collapse and algorithmic bias frameworks

key_claim: Mixing ~30% high-quality rephrased synthetic data with natural web text can speed up pre-training 5-10x, but pre-training on rephrased synthetic data alone is not faster than on natural web text.
methodology: Large-scale empirical investigation training >1000 LLMs (100M-3B parameters) from scratch using scaling laws, comparing natural web data, rephrased synthetic data, generated textbooks, and mixtures under a unified protocol
limitation: Limited to three synthetic data types; relied heavily on perplexity/loss without human evaluations or safety assessments; examined only a single pre-training stage without temporal effects.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the natural web data itself suffers from information degradation over time or whether physically-verified human behavioral data provides distinct training signals.
theoretical_framework: Scaling laws for LLM pre-training with synthetic data mixtures

key_claim: Human training data properties such as initial diversity and quality significantly modulate the magnitude of distribution shifts (model collapse) in recursive LLM training loops.
methodology: Recursive fine-tuning chains of 20 iterations across multiple 1-2B parameter LLMs (LLaMA-3.2-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, SmolLM-1.7B, Falcon3-1B-Base) with exhaustive manipulation of dataset properties and regression analyses
limitation: Experimental design is simplified vs. real-world settings; recursive fine-tuning uses linear chains rather than networks; no human-in-the-loop; limited to small (1-2B parameter) models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data (as opposed to web-scraped text) provides more resilient training signals against recursive distribution shift.
theoretical_framework: Model collapse framework with regression analysis of dataset property effects on distribution shift dynamics

key_claim: Human training data properties such as initial diversity and quality significantly modulate the magnitude of distribution shifts (model collapse) in recursive LLM training loops.
methodology: Recursive fine-tuning chains of 20 iterations across multiple 1-2B parameter LLMs (LLaMA-3.2-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, SmolLM-1.7B, Falcon3-1B-Base) with exhaustive manipulation of dataset properties and regression analyses
limitation: Experimental design is simplified vs. real-world settings; recursive fine-tuning uses linear chains rather than networks; no human-in-the-loop; limited to small (1-2B parameter) models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data (as opposed to web-scraped text) provides more resilient training signals against recursive distribution shift.
theoretical_framework: Model collapse framework with regression analysis of dataset property effects on distribution shift dynamics

key_claim: Recursive curation in self-consuming generative models yields three convergence regimes: consensus collapse, lowest common denominator compromise, and owner-led refinement, depending on preference alignment between curators.
methodology: Formal analysis of a two-stage curation mechanism based on the Bradley-Terry model, modeling alignment as a dynamic interaction between a Model Owner and Public User with social choice theory.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the quality or diversity of the underlying web-scraped training data affects the convergence regimes, nor whether physically-verified behavioral data could prevent diversity collapse.
theoretical_framework: Two-stage recursive Bradley-Terry curation mechanism analyzed via social choice theory and mechanism design

key_claim: BitMark embeds robust watermarks during bitwise autoregressive image generation to enable detection of AI-generated content and mitigate model collapse from training on synthetic data.
methodology: Designing a watermarking method that shifts bitwise token representations toward detectable patterns during generation in Infinity-2B and Instella models, with robustness evaluation against image manipulations.
limitation: Detection accuracy degrades with large rotation angles (56.8% TPR@1%FPR at ±50°), and the soft biasing parameter δ requires tuning as values exceeding 3 cause noticeable quality degradation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether watermarking alone is sufficient to prevent information degradation in web-scraped corpora or whether non-synthetic human behavioral data provides qualitatively different training signals.

key_claim: Constitutional AI improves harmlessness of Llama 3-8B by 40% but reduces helpfulness by 9% and reveals model collapse issues in smaller models during self-improvement.
methodology: Replicating Anthropic's Constitutional AI pipeline on Llama 3-8B using DPO instead of PPO, with supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning from AI feedback, evaluated on MTBenchmark.
limitation: Smaller models suffer model collapse during self-improvement because their output quality is insufficient for effective self-fine-tuning, unlike larger models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the observed model collapse in smaller models is exacerbated by reliance on synthetic or web-scraped data versus high-quality human-generated behavioral data.

key_claim: AI models can autonomously generate new knowledge beyond human-level training data by interacting with environments and optimizing unbounded numeric rewards rather than relying on human-curated benchmarks.
methodology: Proposing a framework with three AI agents (Environment, Strategy, and a collaborative system) that generate, test, and refine strategies in containerized environments, using empirical filtering of self-produced data for retraining.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether self-produced synthetic data suffers the same entropy decline and diversity loss as web-scraped data, nor whether physically-verified human behavioral signals remain necessary for grounding.
theoretical_framework: Generalising agent framework with autonomous dataset generation via empirical filtering and unbounded numeric reward optimization

key_claim: AI-generated content causes Retrieval Collapse: 67% pool contamination erodes source diversity while BM25 allows 19-24% adversarial document infiltration, though LLM rankers show resilience.
methodology: Controlled experiments injecting SEO-style and adversarial AI-generated content into retrieval pipelines, measuring Pool/Exposure/Citation Contamination Rates and Precision@10 across BM25 and LLM rankers
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how retrieval collapse feeds back into LLM training data pipelines, compounding information degradation over successive web-scraping cycles.
theoretical_framework: Retrieval Collapse (two-stage framework: Dominance then System Corruption)

key_claim: AI-generated responses are highly prevalent in MOOC student submissions, identifiable through textual analysis metrics applied to over 4045 responses.
methodology: Large-scale textual analysis of 4045+ student responses from an introductory IoT MOOC, employing multiple metrics to distinguish AI-generated from human-generated text
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how AI-generated educational content recycled into web-scraped training corpora degrades diversity and authenticity of future training data.

key_claim: AI-generated content causes Retrieval Collapse: 67% pool contamination erodes source diversity while BM25 allows 19-24% adversarial document infiltration, though LLM rankers show resilience.
methodology: Controlled experiments injecting SEO-style and adversarial AI-generated content into retrieval pipelines, measuring Pool/Exposure/Citation Contamination Rates and Precision@10 across BM25 and LLM rankers
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how retrieval collapse feeds back into LLM training data pipelines, compounding information degradation over successive web-scraping cycles.
theoretical_framework: Retrieval Collapse (two-stage framework: Dominance then System Corruption)

key_claim: Hyperrealistic AI-generated wildlife images and videos can distort public perceptions of species diversity, threatening conservation efforts.
methodology: Conceptual analysis of threats posed by generative AI wildlife media, drawing on social media influence, anthropomorphism, and nature disconnection literatures
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not quantify how AI-generated wildlife content displaces authentic observational data in web-scraped corpora used for training ecological or multimodal models.

key_claim: A fine-tuned Turkish BERT model achieves 97.08% F1 score in detecting AI-rewritten news content in Turkish media, revealing consistent cross-source and temporally stable detection across 2023-2026.
methodology: Fine-tuning a Turkish-specific BERT model on a purpose-built dataset of 3,600 labeled articles from three editorially distinct Turkish news outlets, with large-scale external application to 3,500+ unseen articles.
limitation: Only three media outlets were used, the dataset of 3,600 samples is modest by fine-tuning standards, and AI-rewritten text generation may lack consistency across runs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the proliferation of AI-generated or AI-rewritten news content feeds back into web-scraped training corpora, potentially accelerating information degradation.
dataset_or_benchmark: 3,600 labeled Turkish news articles from three outlets

key_claim: A supervised detection framework applied to 20 leading Russian online media outlets reveals the prevalence of unmarked AI-generated content in Russian-language news.
methodology: Supervised binary text classification using fine-tuned models applied to a large corpus of news texts collected from 20 top Russian online media outlets (ranked by SimilarWeb traffic) between March and May 2025.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how AI-generated content in Russian news media may contaminate web-scraped training data and contribute to diversity loss or entropy decline in future model training.
dataset_or_benchmark: Corpus from 20 leading Russian online media outlets (SimilarWeb-ranked)

key_claim: The Dead Internet Theory is substantiated by growing evidence that bots and AI-generated content are increasingly dominating online interactions, fundamentally altering the internet's structure.
methodology: Critical literature review and qualitative investigation of the Dead Internet Theory, examining evidence from bot prevalence reports and AI-generated content trends.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not quantitatively measure how bot-dominated and AI-generated web content degrades the quality and diversity of web-scraped training corpora used for language model pretraining.

key_claim: Approximately 12% of images shared on X during the 2024 U.S. Presidential Election are AI-generated, with 10% of users responsible for sharing 80% of all AI-generated images.
methodology: Large-scale analysis of ~2.5 million images from Twitter/X related to the 2024 U.S. Election (July–September 2024), using GPT-4o for AI-generated image detection, with characterization of superspreader behavior and emotional reception analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the concentration of AI-generated political images on social media contributes to the degradation of authentic human behavioral signals in web-scraped training datasets.
dataset_or_benchmark: 2024 U.S. Election Twitter/X dataset (2.5M images, 414K users)

key_claim: Synthetic data contamination causes catastrophic performance loss in online continual learning, and entropy-based sample selection with real-synthetic similarity maximization (ESRM) substantially mitigates this degradation.
methodology: Proposed ESRM framework combining Entropy Selection and Real-synthetic similarity Maximization with self-distillation, evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, and ImageNet-100 contaminated with SDXL-generated images at varying contamination ratios.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how synthetic data contamination in text corpora (rather than image datasets) degrades language model training or whether similar entropy-based filtering could preserve authentic human-generated text signals.

key_claim: AI-generated content labels on social media show attenuated or stable effects on perceived accuracy and sharing intention over 18 days but induce a small negative affective shift ('emotional tax') on true content.
methodology: Two-wave online experiment (N=400 Wave 1, N=256 Wave 2, 18-day interval) with health posts varying in veracity and content type, measuring perceived accuracy, credibility, sharing intention, and affect.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the proliferation of AI-generated content changes the underlying diversity or informational quality of training data scraped from social platforms.

key_claim: AI-generated content labels on social media show attenuated or stable effects on perceived accuracy and sharing intention over 18 days but induce a small negative affective shift ('emotional tax') on true content.
methodology: Two-wave online experiment (N=400 Wave 1, N=256 Wave 2, 18-day interval) with health posts varying in veracity and content type, measuring perceived accuracy, credibility, sharing intention, and affect.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the proliferation of AI-generated content changes the underlying diversity or informational quality of training data scraped from social platforms.

key_claim: A preregistered experiment across 27 European countries examines how people perceive and intend to share AI-generated versus human-generated fake and real news headlines about the Russo-Ukrainian war.
methodology: Preregistered experiment embedded in a large-scale web survey across 27 European countries; participants evaluated eight news headlines (4 AI-generated, 4 human-generated; split real/fake) for perceived veracity and sharing intention.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how AI-generated misinformation, once shared and scraped, degrades the diversity and authenticity of web-scale training corpora used for future models.

key_claim: SVM outperforms BERT variants for detecting AI-generated product reviews in Tamil while ALBERT performs better for Malayalam.
methodology: Classification of AI-generated vs human-written product reviews using ALBERT, IndicBERT, and SVM on Dravidian language datasets
limitation: ALBERT's 512 token limit is restrictive for longer AI text; models may overfit to one AI generator (e.g., ChatGPT) and fail on newer models; SVM lacks sequential information and struggles when AI and human text are very similar.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the proliferation of AI-generated reviews in low-resource languages degrades the authenticity and diversity of web-scraped training corpora.

key_claim: The introduction of AI-generated content on Pixiv significantly increased content creation volumes while revealing notable behavioral differences between AIGC creators/consumers and human-content creators/consumers.
methodology: Longitudinal analysis of the Pixiv platform ecosystem examining content creation, consumption, and engagement metrics before and after AIGC introduction across over 100 million submissions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not quantify how the surge of AI-generated artistic content displaces authentic human creative expression or degrades the diversity and entropy of image-based training data sourced from social platforms.

key_claim: Labeling headlines as AI-generated lowers perceived accuracy and sharing willingness regardless of actual veracity or authorship, driven by assumptions of full AI automation.
methodology: Two pre-registered online experiments with US and UK participants (N=4,976) testing effects of AI-generated labels on headline perception
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how AI-content labeling affects the downstream curation and filtering of web-scraped training data or whether skepticism toward AI labels reduces authentic human behavioral signals in shared content.

key_claim: Labeling headlines as AI-generated lowers perceived accuracy and sharing willingness regardless of actual veracity or authorship, driven by assumptions of full AI automation.
methodology: Two pre-registered online experiments with US and UK participants (N=4,976) testing effects of AI-generated labels on headline perception
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how AI-content labeling affects the downstream curation and filtering of web-scraped training data or whether skepticism toward AI labels reduces authentic human behavioral signals in shared content.

key_claim: AI ecosystems recursively training on AI-generated content trigger a degenerative informational feedback loop causing irreversible loss of diversity, accuracy, and long-tail knowledge structure.
methodology: Synthesis of emerging evidence from model collapse research, open-source contamination cycles, and benchmark erosion analysis
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not propose empirical methods to measure or quantify the rate of information degradation in web-scraped training corpora over time.
theoretical_framework: Civilization Physics — Entropy Dynamics framework analogizing synthetic collapse to biological inbreeding

key_claim: A watermark can be embedded in LLM-generated text with negligible quality impact and detected efficiently without access to model API or parameters.
methodology: Watermarking framework that selects randomized 'green' token lists before generation and softly promotes their use during sampling, with z-statistic-based detection
limitation: Careful implementation and instruction tuning against generative attacks may be required for very large models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how watermarking could help distinguish authentic human-generated web content from synthetic content to prevent training data contamination.
theoretical_framework: Statistical hypothesis testing framework using z-statistics for watermark detection

key_claim: Paraphrasing with an 11B parameter model easily evades current AI-generated text detectors, but retrieval-based detection is an effective and scalable defense.
methodology: Built DIPPER, an 11B parameter paraphrase generation model, to stress test watermarking and outlier detection methods; proposed retrieval-based detection searching a corpus of prior LLM generations
limitation: Retrieval-based detection has privacy risks from exposing all LLM responses and requires LLM providers to store generated outputs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the inability to reliably detect AI-generated text contributes to progressive contamination and diversity loss in web-scraped training corpora.

key_claim: AI-generated code can be detected by targeted masking perturbation of high-perplexity regions paired with comprehensive scoring using fine-tuned CodeBERT.
methodology: Targeted masking perturbation where high-perplexity code regions are more intensely masked, then filled by fine-tuned CodeBERT, with comprehensive scoring to distinguish AI-generated vs human-written code
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider how undetected AI-generated code entering open-source repositories could degrade training data diversity for future code generation models.

key_claim: Hybrid detection approaches combining multiple modalities achieve up to 92% accuracy in identifying synthetic content, outperforming single-modality methods.
methodology: Systematic review of detection methodologies across text, image, audio, and video modalities including stylometric analysis, watermarking, pixel prediction, dual-stream networks, ML models, blockchain, and hybrid approaches
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the proliferation of synthetic content in web-scraped corpora degrades training data quality or causes tail collapse in downstream models.

key_claim: The detectability of AI-generated text depends on multiple interacting factors including generation method, domain, and detection approach, making universal detection highly challenging.
methodology: Survey and synthesis of state-of-the-art AIGT detection approaches including watermarking, statistical and stylistic analysis, and machine learning classification, with review of existing datasets
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how undetected AI-generated text accumulating in web-scraped corpora may cause information degradation and entropy decline in future training data.

key_claim: Digital watermarking research has evolved from copyright protection to fake news detection, with over 130 survey papers mapping this expanding field.
methodology: Meta-survey of 64 selected research works summarizing notable survey papers in digital media watermarking, combined with requirements analysis for watermarking-based fake news detection
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider whether watermarking techniques could help distinguish authentic human-generated data from synthetic content in large-scale web-scraped training corpora.

key_claim: RoBERTa-based AI classifiers can identify ChatGPT-generated ESL essays but exhibit inconsistent detection accuracy across the dataset.
methodology: Cross-disciplinary descriptive study evaluating two RoBERTa-based classifiers on a dataset of 240 human-written and ChatGPT-generated essays in ESL contexts
limitation: Detection accuracy of RoBERTa-based classifiers was inconsistent across the dataset, limiting their reliability for controlling AI-assisted plagiarism.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine the broader risk that undetected AI-generated text entering educational and web corpora contributes to diversity loss and information degradation in training data.

key_claim: AI-enabled content generation in education should be framed as 'misattributed co-authorship' requiring new responsible human-AI collaboration frameworks.
methodology: Literature review and conceptual framing of ChatGPT educational misuse as misattributed co-authorship
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the quality or provenance of AI training data affects the generated content used in academic cheating.
theoretical_framework: Misattributed co-authorship framework for human-AI collaboration

key_claim: Adversarial learning that jointly trains a paraphraser and detector produces AI-text detectors robust to LLM-based paraphrasing with strong transferability across LLMs.
methodology: Adversarial training framework (RADAR) jointly training a T5-large paraphraser and a detector, evaluated on 8 LLMs and 4 datasets
limitation: RADAR may show degraded detection performance against native LLM-generated texts without paraphrasing compared to the best existing method, and cannot always make correct predictions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how degradation or homogenization of web-scraped training data affects the distinguishability of AI-generated versus human-generated text.
theoretical_framework: Adversarial learning framework for robust AI-text detection

key_claim: Simple manipulation techniques reduce GenAI text detector accuracy by 17.4%, making them unreliable for determining academic integrity violations.
methodology: Comparative evaluation of six major GenAI text detectors on 805 machine-generated texts modified with simple evasion techniques
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the declining diversity or entropy of web-scraped training data affects the detectability of AI-generated text over time.

key_claim: Recursive paraphrasing attacks can break watermarking, neural network, zero-shot, and retrieval-based AI-text detectors, and spoofing attacks can increase their type-I errors.
methodology: Stress-testing four classes of AI-text detectors using recursive paraphrasing with DIPPER and LLaMA-2-7B-Chat, evaluated via automated metrics, MTurk human studies, and theoretical AUROC bounds via total variation distance
limitation: Authors acknowledge the study focuses on specific paraphrasing-based attacks and that theoretical bounds rely on total variation distance which may not capture all practical attack scenarios.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider how information degradation in web-scraped corpora or loss of authentic human behavioral signals may fundamentally alter the statistical separability of human and AI text.
theoretical_framework: Theoretical connection between best possible detector AUROC and total variation distance between human and AI text distributions

key_claim: A semi-fragile watermarking technique can authenticate media by embedding invisible messages robust to benign operations but fragile to malicious manipulations.
methodology: Deep learning-based semi-fragile watermarking embedded in image pixels for proactive media authentication
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how watermarking or provenance techniques could help distinguish authentic human-generated training data from synthetic or degraded web-scraped content.

key_claim: Recursively training language models on synthetic text generated by predecessors leads to a consistent decline in lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity across generations.
methodology: Recursive finetuning experiments on multiple NLG tasks (abstractive summarization, scientific abstract generation, story generation) with novel lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity metrics
limitation: Experiments are conducted exclusively in English; linguistic diversity metrics rely on language-specific tokenization, dependency parsing, and sentence embeddings that may not generalize to low-resource languages.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether incorporating physically-verified authentic human behavioral data could counteract the observed diversity collapse in recursive synthetic training.

key_claim: Bangla literary corpora exhibit greater lexical diversity and morphological richness than much larger newspaper corpora, and injecting modest literary data into newspaper corpora enriches them lexically.
methodology: Corpus-driven comparative analysis using type-token ratio, hapax legomena ratio, bigram diversity, Zipf's law adherence, entropy, and redundancy metrics on Vācaspati (literary) and IndicCorp (newspaper) corpora
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the dominance of web-scraped newspaper text in NLP training leads to systematic diversity loss compared to richer authentic human literary sources.

key_claim: An empirical functional relation between word entropy and type-token ratio exists across corpora and languages, analytically derivable from Zipf's and Heaps' laws in the large-text limit.
methodology: Quantitative analysis of entropy and type-token ratio across six gigaword corpora in English, Spanish, and Turkish (books, news, tweets), with analytical derivation from Zipf's and Heaps' laws
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how these entropy-TTR relationships change when corpora contain increasing proportions of synthetic or machine-generated text, relevant to detecting information degradation in training data.
theoretical_framework: Analytical framework relating entropy and type-token ratio via Zipf's law and Heaps' law

key_claim: There is a consistent functional relationship between Shannon entropy and type-token ratio across gigaword corpora in multiple languages, derivable from Zipf's law and Heaps' law.
methodology: Empirical analysis of entropy and type-token ratio across six gigaword corpora (books, news, tweets) in English, Spanish, and Turkish, with asymptotic derivations from Zipf's law
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the entropy–TTR relationship changes over time in web-scraped corpora or whether synthetic/AI-generated text exhibits different entropy–TTR dynamics indicative of diversity loss.
theoretical_framework: Zipf's law and Heaps' law asymptotic analysis linking Shannon entropy to type-token ratio

key_claim: Denoising cross-entropy loss outperforms score entropy for training discrete diffusion language models, and a new KL divergence bound enables improved perplexity estimation.
methodology: Derivation of three new theorems on KL divergence in continuous-time discrete Markov chain frameworks, introduction of a new transition-rate matrix for token correction, and empirical comparison of loss functions on language modeling benchmarks
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how training data quality or diversity degradation in web-scraped corpora affects the learned distributions of discrete diffusion language models.
theoretical_framework: Continuous-time discrete Markov chain (CTMC) framework for discrete diffusion models

key_claim: An NLP-based classifier can distinguish AI-generated phishing messages from both legitimate emails and human-written phishing attempts.
methodology: Assembly of a balanced dataset from authentic emails, human-written phishing samples, and generative-AI-produced phishing messages, followed by NLP-based classification
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the proliferation of AI-generated phishing text in web corpora contributes to information degradation and reduced authenticity of training data.

key_claim: Traditional corpus-based complexity indices (Type-Token Ratio and word-level Entropy) are more prone to fluctuation with corpus size and content changes than newer indices (Word Information Density and Lexical Diversity) across 47 languages.
methodology: Systematic robustness testing of four complexity indices (TTR, word-level Entropy, Word Information Density, Lexical Diversity) estimated from raw texts across 47 languages under varying corpus size and content conditions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not assess whether web-scraped corpora exhibit systematic bias in complexity indices compared to curated or authentic human-produced corpora, relevant to detecting diversity loss in training data.
theoretical_framework: Cross-linguistic complexity measurement framework using Type-Token Ratio, Entropy, Word Information Density, and Lexical Diversity

key_claim: Adaptive watermarking that targets high-entropy tokens and scales logits based on semantic embeddings improves text quality, robustness, and security over fixed green/red list approaches.
methodology: Entropy-based adaptive watermark insertion using an auxiliary model to selectively watermark high-entropy token distributions, with semantically-scaled logit adjustment
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how watermarking interacts with the degradation of training data quality when watermarked LLM outputs are recursively scraped and re-used as training data.

key_claim: Recursive self-training acts as a sharpening operator on the data distribution, causing systematic tail collapse where rare and difficult samples vanish across model generations.
methodology: Formalization of recursive training as iterative distributional transformation with controlled experiments on discrete distributions, continuous models, and language models
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not explicitly evaluate whether incorporating physically-verified authentic human behavioral data can restore or preserve tail probability mass lost during recursive synthetic training.
theoretical_framework: Loss distribution collapse framework with a tail invariance principle

key_claim: Recursive self-training acts as a sharpening operator on the data distribution, causing systematic tail collapse where rare and difficult samples vanish across model generations.
methodology: Formalization of recursive training as iterative distributional transformation with controlled experiments on discrete distributions, continuous models, and language models
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not explicitly evaluate whether incorporating physically-verified authentic human behavioral data can restore or preserve tail probability mass lost during recursive synthetic training.
theoretical_framework: Loss distribution collapse framework with a tail invariance principle

key_claim: Lexical diversity in Czech L2 texts increases with writer proficiency, with significant differences between Slavic and non-Slavic native speakers.
methodology: Corpus analysis using Moving Average Type-Token Ratio (MATTR) to measure lexical diversity across CEFR proficiency levels (A1–C1)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how lexical diversity metrics from authentic human L2 writing compare to synthetic text generated by LLMs, nor whether such human diversity signals degrade in web-scraped corpora.

key_claim: CurveMark combines probability curvature analysis with dynamic semantic watermarking to detect AI-generated text while maximizing mutual information between text sources and observable features.
methodology: Dual-channel detection framework combining probability curvature analysis, dynamic semantic watermarking, and Bayesian multi-hypothesis detection for AI text identification
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how degradation of web-scraped training data or declining text diversity affects the distinguishability of AI-generated versus human-written content over time.
theoretical_framework: Information-theoretic dual-channel detection with Bayesian multi-hypothesis framework

key_claim: Machine-translated training data consistently underperforms native English data, with lexical diversity predicting performance in low-data settings and syntactic similarity to English predicting performance at larger scales.
methodology: Training small English language models on English text machine-translated from 24 typologically diverse source languages, evaluating perplexity on native English text and linguistic acceptability judgments
limitation: Translates into English (high-resource) rather than low-resource languages, representing an upper bound on translated data quality; does not fully disentangle source language typological properties from corpus properties produced by translation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how machine-translated text contributes to diversity loss or entropy decline in large-scale web-scraped corpora used for LLM pre-training.

key_claim: Integral probability metrics with bounded test classes can be evaluated from finite samples with multiplicative and additive approximation errors, establishing formal evaluability conditions for generative models.
methodology: Mathematical analysis of statistical evaluability for generative model metrics, studying integral probability metrics (IPMs) and Rényi divergences under finite sample conditions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether evaluation metrics can detect information degradation or diversity loss in generative model outputs trained on web-scraped data.
theoretical_framework: Theoretical framework for statistical evaluability using integral probability metrics (IPMs) and Rényi divergences

key_claim: LLM-prompted authorship impersonations using GPT-4o do not evade state-of-the-art authorship verification methods across multiple prompting strategies and verification algorithms.
methodology: Generated impersonation texts using GPT-4o under four prompting conditions across three genres, evaluated against non-neural (n-gram tracing, ranking-based impostors) and neural authorship verification methods
limitation: Experiments simulate only entry-level attackers using straightforward prompting; more sophisticated adversarial tactics such as iterative refinement using AV feedback or parameter-efficient fine-tuning were not explored.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether authentic human behavioral writing signals degrade as LLM-generated text proliferates in web training data, potentially reducing distinctiveness of human authorial style.

key_claim: Augmenting reverse KL with forward KL at high-entropy teacher tokens preserves generation diversity and yields consistent gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
methodology: Entropy-aware on-policy distillation (EOPD) that selectively applies forward KL divergence at high-entropy teacher token positions during on-policy knowledge distillation, evaluated on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether entropy-aware distillation can mitigate diversity loss arising from low-diversity or degraded web-scraped training corpora rather than from the distillation objective itself.
theoretical_framework: Entropy-aware switching between reverse KL and forward KL divergence objectives based on teacher token entropy threshold

key_claim: Projecting intermediate hidden states into vocabulary space via Logit Lens and enforcing symmetric divergence alignment enables students to faithfully mirror teachers' internal reasoning, outperforming standard KD.
methodology: Feature-based knowledge distillation using Logit Lens projection of intermediate hidden states into vocabulary space with Jensen-Shannon Divergence alignment, evaluated on GPT-2 and Llama architectures.
limitation: The primary limitation is the computational overhead in training due to projecting intermediate hidden states via the unembedding matrix.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether intermediate-layer alignment can compensate for degraded or low-diversity training data, nor how data quality affects the fidelity of distilled representations.
theoretical_framework: Symmetric divergence (JSD/Jeffreys Divergence) alignment of Logit Lens-projected intermediate representations

key_claim: ProGRPO's confidence-aware advantage reweighting mechanism mitigates entropy collapse in RLVR training, improving Pass@1 by 5.7% and Pass@32 by 13.8% over GRPO on Qwen2.5-7B.
methodology: Proposed ProGRPO algorithm with Low-Probability Token Length Normalization and Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) applied to GRPO-based RLVR training, evaluated on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks with Qwen2.5-7B.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the mode collapse and entropy decline during RL post-training are exacerbated by low-diversity or degraded web-scraped pretraining data.
theoretical_framework: Generative probability-based advantage reweighting within Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)

key_claim: ForgetMark achieves perfect fingerprint verification on 7-8B LLMs with minimal utility loss and higher stealth than backdoor baselines by encoding provenance via targeted unlearning of compact key-value sets.
methodology: Targeted unlearning with LoRA adapters trained to suppress preset key-value responses, using predictive-entropy ranking for key selection and GPT-4 as auxiliary assistant model; evaluated on Mistral-7B, LLaMA-3-8B, and Qwen2.5-7B with model merging robustness tests.
limitation: Partial recovery of fingerprint under sustained incremental fine-tuning is acknowledged as a limitation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how training data provenance or quality (e.g., web-scraped vs. authentic human data) affects the robustness of fingerprint embedding and verification.
theoretical_framework: Targeted unlearning framework with predictive-entropy-ranked key-value fingerprint sets and likelihood/semantic aggregation for verification

key_claim: The minimum of Renyi entropy coincides with the true number of topics in labelled collections, enabling optimal topic number selection.
methodology: Renyi entropy-based analysis of four topic models (pLSA, BigARTM, LDA with Gibbs sampling, VLDA) on two labelled collections, inspired by statistical physics concepts.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how topic model performance degrades when trained on web-scraped corpora exhibiting information degradation or reduced diversity.
theoretical_framework: Renyi entropy framework treating topical structure as an information statistical system in a non-equilibrium state

key_claim: Topic modeling output exhibits self-similar behavior under variation of cluster number, enabling renormalization combined with Renyi entropy for quick optimal topic search.
methodology: Renormalization technique from statistical physics applied to pLSA, VLDA, and GLDA topic models, combined with Renyi entropy approach, tested on two datasets.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the self-similar properties of topic distributions change when underlying corpora suffer from tail collapse or entropy decline.
theoretical_framework: Renormalization group analysis combined with Renyi entropy for topic models

key_claim: MDI adaptation of n-gram language models can be computed in linear-time complexity per iteration by exploiting backoff structure and hierarchical training.
methodology: Generalized Iterative Scaling algorithm leveraging backoff structure of n-gram models and hierarchical training, evaluated on domain adaptation across speech corpora.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider how domain adaptation performance is affected when out-of-domain training data suffers from diversity loss or information degradation.

key_claim: CCI4.0, a 35TB bilingual pretraining dataset with model-based quality scoring and 4.5 billion CoT templates, improves LLM reasoning over existing datasets.
methodology: Multi-stage data processing pipeline including deduplication, multi-classifier quality scoring, fluency filtering, Chain-of-Thought synthesis, and privacy/toxicity handling applied to Chinese web corpus and Nemotron-CC English data.
limitation: Currently supports only Chinese and English; large scale may be impractical for resource-limited researchers; privacy-preserving and toxicity filtering cannot guarantee complete removal of problematic content.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze whether its web-scraped sources exhibit tail collapse or entropy decline, nor whether physically-verified human behavioral data could provide complementary training signals.

key_claim: Enriching Romanian pretraining datasets with multi-level metadata (topic, format, educational level) and filtering substantially improves Llama-2 pretraining performance for underrepresented languages.
methodology: Trained a lightweight multitask classifier on LLM-annotated Romanian texts from FineWeb2 to perform multi-level filtering (educational value, topic, format) and generated high-quality pretraining datasets.
limitation: Filtering reduces dataset size below what Chinchilla scaling law requires for optimal large model training; limited format diversity in training data affects classifier accuracy.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether web-scraped Romanian data suffers from systematic information degradation or tail collapse compared to authentic human-generated Romanian text.

key_claim: Yi models achieve near GPT-3.5 performance primarily due to data quality engineering rather than architectural innovation, with smaller models on higher-quality data outperforming larger models on lower-quality data.
methodology: Pretrained 6B and 34B transformer language models from scratch on 3.1T tokens of heavily curated data, extended to chat, 200K long-context, depth-upscaled, and vision-language variants; finetuned with next-word prediction loss on responses only using AdamW optimizer with NEFTune.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze whether the web-crawled pretraining data exhibits diversity loss or tail collapse, nor whether physically-verified human behavioral data could provide complementary training signals.

key_claim: Classifier-based Quality Filtering improves downstream task performance not by making data resemble the high-quality set, but by implicitly removing documents most typical of the low-quality set.
methodology: Trained 350M parameter models on CQF-filtered datasets with varying high-quality reference sets and filtering thresholds; analyzed filtering behavior through data-conditioning lens using semi-synthetic text datasets derived from RedPajama-V2.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether CQF's implicit removal of 'bad' data systematically eliminates rare or tail-distributed content, potentially accelerating information degradation and diversity loss in filtered web corpora.

key_claim: Synthetic pretraining data generated via document-grounded, persona-grounded, and topic-grounded techniques at 540B tokens scale can substantially improve LLM performance for low-resource Indic languages.
methodology: Built a scalable synthetic data generation pipeline using multilingual LLMs with five generation techniques (document-grounded, persona-grounded, topic-grounded, math/reasoning, translation) across 10 Indic languages, with an automated quality evaluation pipeline including language ID, perplexity, and quality classifiers.
limitation: English named entities and noun references impact perplexity scores for some languages; certain languages like Tamil and Bengali show high rates of fluency issues; Malayalam exhibits disproportionately high low-quality rates due to repetitions and poor coherence.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not evaluate whether synthetic data introduces homogenization or entropy decline relative to authentic human-generated Indic text, nor whether it can capture the diversity of real social and cultural behavioral signals.

key_claim: Small proxy model experiments for data recipe evaluation are brittle because conclusions about data quality can flip with minor learning rate adjustments.
methodology: Systematic hyperparameter sweeps (primarily learning rate) on small proxy models across multiple data recipes, with theoretical analysis of optimal learning rate sensitivity to data distribution.
limitation: Focuses only on single-epoch LLM pretraining; does not address multi-epoch or curriculum learning scenarios; proxy model techniques for those settings remain open.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether proxy model brittleness differentially affects evaluation of web-scraped versus authentic human behavioral data, or whether tail-collapse artifacts are amplified by suboptimal proxy configurations.
theoretical_framework: Formal analysis of dataset-dependent optimal learning rates under continuous data distributions with width-m neural networks

key_claim: A model-based HTML parser (MinerU-HTML) yields extraction quality improvements that produce LLM pretraining gains of 1.08pp over heuristic-based extraction with identical post-processing.
methodology: Sequence labeling (content classification) on simplified HTML blocks using a trained model, constructing a 7.3T-token corpus (AICC) from Common Crawl; evaluated via a new benchmark (WebMainBench) and downstream LLM training.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze whether improved HTML extraction mitigates information degradation such as tail collapse or diversity loss in web-scraped corpora, nor whether extraction improvements recover underrepresented authentic human content.

key_claim: Jointly optimizing data quality and diversity via a parameterized sampling function (QuaDMix) outperforms sequential quality-filtering-then-rebalancing approaches for LLM pretraining.
methodology: Parameterized sampling function controlling quality and diversity; proxy model experiments across sampled parameter configurations; regression model trained on proxy results to identify optimal parameters; large-scale LLM pretraining with optimal data.
limitation: Parameter space may produce redundant functions introducing regression model uncertainty; random search in 200+ dimensional space likely yields local minima; systematic improvement of small proxy model transferability remains unclear.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the quality-diversity trade-off differs for web-scraped text versus physically-verified human behavioral data, nor whether diversity optimization can counteract tail collapse in web corpora.

key_claim: Sample-wise bottom-up data mixing that jointly considers per-sample quality and global cross-domain diversity achieves comparable accuracy to domain-wise methods with 1.9x fewer training steps.
methodology: Global cross-domain clustering for diversity evaluation combined with multi-dimensional sample-level quality scoring; bottom-up sampling that coordinates quality and diversity at the individual sample level; experiments on SlimPajama.
limitation: Experiments conducted exclusively on SlimPajama; optimal hyperparameters may not transfer to other datasets; users must perform tailored hyperparameter tuning for their own data.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether sample-wise mixing can detect or compensate for entropy decline and tail collapse in web-scraped data, nor whether authentic human social behavioral data requires distinct mixing strategies.

key_claim: RedPajama provides over 100 trillion tokens as the largest fully open and transparent dataset for pretraining, enabling models that outperform those trained on other large-scale corpora.
methodology: Large-scale web data curation from CommonCrawl with deduplication, quality filtering, and document-level processing across multiple snapshots; training RedPajama-INCITE 3B and 7B models to validate data quality.
limitation: Models trained are relatively small, limiting exploration of how filters scale to larger models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how information degradation across CommonCrawl snapshots over time (e.g., declining content diversity or entropy) affects downstream model quality.

key_claim: Rephrasing high-quality web documents into synthetic data via BeyondWeb outperforms state-of-the-art synthetic pretraining datasets by increasing per-token information density without requiring novel content generation.
methodology: Rephrasing-driven synthetic data generation framework that curates billions of high-quality web documents and diversifies them across styles and formats using large-scale LLM inference on H100 clusters; evaluation at trillion-token pretraining scale.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether synthetic rephrasing perpetuates or amplifies biases and information homogeneity already present in degraded web-scraped source data.

key_claim: MetaCLIP's metadata-driven curation and balancing of raw CommonCrawl data outperforms CLIP's proprietary data source without reliance on any external model.
methodology: Metadata-based curation pipeline using sub-string matching against metadata entries, English language identification, de-duplication, and distribution balancing applied to CommonCrawl image-text pairs at scales up to 10.7B pairs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how tail collapse or declining diversity in web-scraped CommonCrawl snapshots over time affects the quality and representativeness of curated vision-language training data.

key_claim: Continual pre-training of LLMs on junk social media data causes persistent, multifaceted cognitive decline in reasoning, safety, and long-context understanding that resists large-scale post-hoc tuning.
methodology: Controlled experiments using two orthogonal operationalizations of junk data (engagement degree M1 and semantic quality M2) from real Twitter/X corpora; continual pre-training of 4 LLMs with matched token scale; effect sizes measured via Hedges' g across reasoning, safety, and personality benchmarks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether physically-verified authentic human behavioral data can serve as a remedial training signal to reverse or mitigate the cognitive decline caused by junk web data exposure.

key_claim: Balanced pretraining creates latent capabilities best activated by SFT on small, hard examples, while scaling SFT data introduces interference that erodes pretrained structure.
methodology: Theoretical analysis of transformers trained on an in-context weight prediction task for linear regression, with formal characterization of SFT and RL loss landscapes.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how degradation or diversity loss in web-scraped pretraining corpora affects the quality of the latent capabilities that post-training must activate.
theoretical_framework: In-context weight prediction task for linear regression analyzing SFT and RL loss surfaces

key_claim: Curriculum-based pretraining gains are constrained by incompatibility between ascending data quality order and decaying learning rate schedules, which waste the best data.
methodology: Experiments with 0.5B parameter language models comparing curriculum-based data ordering with different learning rate schedules, proposing moderate LR decay combined with model averaging.
limitation: Folding strategy benefits diminish or reverse under higher peak learning rates; the proposed moderate decay plus model averaging approach has not been validated at large scale.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how progressive quality degradation or entropy decline in web-scraped data over time interacts with curriculum and learning rate scheduling.
theoretical_framework: Analysis of coupling between learning rate decay and data quality schedules as implicit importance weighting

key_claim: Front-loading reasoning data into pretraining creates durable foundations that post-training alone cannot recover, with diversity driving pretraining and quality governing SFT.
methodology: Systematic experiments varying the allocation of reasoning data (scale, diversity, quality) between pretraining and supervised fine-tuning stages of LLM development, evaluated on math, science, code, and general reasoning tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the diversity and authenticity of reasoning data sources degrade over time due to web-scraped corpus homogenization or synthetic data contamination.
theoretical_framework: Optimization framework for reasoning data allocation across pretraining and SFT stages

key_claim: CRAW4LLM achieves equivalent LLM pretraining performance to standard crawling while crawling only 21% of URLs by prioritizing pages based on pretraining influence scores.
methodology: Web graph exploration using pretraining influence scores as crawler priority, evaluated on a 900-million-webpage web graph from a commercial search engine index (ClueWeb22).
limitation: Experiments are conducted on a static web graph dataset (ClueWeb22) rather than actual live web crawling; copyright and fair use concerns are not fully resolved; further validation on real crawling infrastructure is needed.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether optimizing crawling for pretraining influence exacerbates tail collapse and diversity loss by systematically deprioritizing rare or niche authentic human content.

key_claim: Organizing pretraining data into four quadrants based on perplexity and perplexity difference yields a 16.8% average improvement on MMLU and CMMLU over random sampling.
methodology: Partitioning pretraining data into four quadrants using PPL and PD metrics from strong and weak reference models, then training LLMs in a four-stage sequence designed to achieve significant loss reductions.
limitation: Data is only split into two parts per dimension; future work should explore finer subdivisions and verify applicability across architectures like Mamba and Mixture of Experts, and extend to broader evaluations including domain-specific and interactive tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the quality or diversity of the underlying web-scraped corpus itself (e.g., tail collapse or entropy decline) affects the efficacy of multi-stage pretraining strategies.

key_claim: Generating synthetic math-informed dialogues from OpenWebMath and mixing them with raw data improves mathematical reasoning in LLMs during pretraining.
methodology: Generating large-scale synthetic conversational data (MIND) from OpenWebMath using multiple dialogue styles, then pretraining LLMs on a mixture of raw and synthetic corpora, evaluated via LM Eval Harness on reasoning benchmarks.
limitation: The TWO PROFESSORS conversational style yields equivalent or worse performance due to zero knowledge gap between participants, leading to surface-level engagement.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the underlying web-scraped mathematical corpus suffers from information degradation or diversity loss that limits the ceiling of synthetic data augmentation.

key_claim: Training on the easiest examples in web-scraped data degrades LLM performance, while perplexity-based pruning retaining harder examples allows models trained on half the data to achieve up to 1.5% improvement.
methodology: Systematic comparison at scale of data pruning methods (perplexity, EL2N, memorization) applied to CommonCrawl-derived pretraining data, evaluated via test perplexity and fine-tuning on GLUE classification tasks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether perplexity-based pruning systematically removes tail-distribution or minority-topic content, potentially exacerbating diversity loss in training corpora.

key_claim: A dimensionless data-quality parameter Q extends the Chinchilla scaling law, showing that higher-quality data allows smaller models and less data to match the performance of larger runs on lower-quality data.
methodology: Extending the Chinchilla scaling law with an effective-sample-size framework, systematically injecting synthetic noise into NMT and causal language modeling experiments to fit a quality-aware scaling law predicting loss as a function of model size, data volume, and data quality.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not model naturally occurring quality degradation in web corpora over time (e.g., tail collapse or entropy decline) as opposed to synthetically injected noise.
theoretical_framework: Quality-aware scaling law extending the Chinchilla framework with effective-sample-size and information-theoretic motivation

key_claim: Model-based filtering is the key technique for assembling high-quality training sets from Common Crawl, enabling DCLM-BASELINE to achieve state-of-the-art language model performance.
methodology: Controlled dataset experiments comparing data curation strategies (deduplication, model-based filtering, data mixing) on a standardized 240T token Common Crawl corpus at model scales from 412M to 7B parameters, evaluated on 53 downstream tasks.
limitation: Compute constraints limited ablations to individual dimensions; could not test all approaches at larger scales or beyond 7B parameters; insufficient exploration of run-to-run variation; most experiments used only one tokenizer (GPT-NeoX).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the underlying Common Crawl corpus suffers from progressive information degradation, diversity loss, or entropy decline over time that filtering alone cannot remedy.

key_claim: LLMs can be aligned to human values with minimal human supervision using principle-driven self-alignment, producing the Dromedary model from scratch with almost no human annotation.
methodology: Four-stage SELF-ALIGN pipeline: topic-guided red-teaming self-instruct for synthetic instruction generation, principle-driven self-alignment with in-context learning, principle engraving via fine-tuning, and verbose cloning to improve helpfulness.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether self-generated synthetic alignment data can substitute for authentic human behavioral signals or whether such self-alignment compounds biases present in web-scraped pretraining data.
theoretical_framework: SELF-ALIGN (principle-driven reasoning framework for LLM alignment)

key_claim: BeaverTails provides separately annotated helpfulness and harmlessness labels for 333,963 QA pairs, enabling more nuanced safety alignment of LLMs via RLHF.
methodology: Crowdsourced annotation of QA pairs with separate safety meta-labels and expert comparison data; applied to content moderation and RLHF fine-tuning experiments.
limitation: Limited demographic diversity among the 70 crowdworkers, whose similar cultural backgrounds could contribute to narrower representation of human preference; classification taxonomy may not capture all safety dimensions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the underlying QA data reflects authentic diverse human social behaviors or is biased by web-scraped content patterns and information degradation.

key_claim: RRHF aligns language models with human feedback by ranking sampled responses using conditional log-probabilities, achieving comparable performance to PPO while being simpler to train and scale.
methodology: Scoring sampled responses from multiple sources via log conditional probabilities and aligning them with human preferences through ranking loss; evaluated on Alpaca models with diverse beam search and top-p sampling against PPO baselines.
limitation: Reward model used as proxy may not fully capture human preference complexity; RRHF requires multiple responses per query increasing GPU usage; may be prone to reward hacking in preliminary experiments.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider whether the quality of alignment is limited by degraded or homogeneous web-scraped training data underlying the base models being aligned.
theoretical_framework: RRHF (Rank Responses to align with Human Feedback)

key_claim: An instructable reward model guided by human-defined principles can align language models with minimal human supervision, achieving superior performance to traditional RLHF approaches.
methodology: Training an instructable reward model with adjustable principles combined with RL and the SELF-ALIGN technique to build Dromedary-2, using only 6 in-context learning exemplars and 31 human-defined principles; evaluated via GPT-4 automatic evaluation on chatbot benchmarks.
limitation: Dromedary-2 occasionally hallucinates unverified information and displays reasoning errors, potentially stemming from inherent limitations of SFT-initialized reward models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the quality or diversity of underlying pretraining web-scraped data affects the synthetic preference generation and self-alignment process.

key_claim: Reinforcement learning is fundamentally a human-in-the-loop paradigm, and human-centric approaches are key to successful RL but have not been adequately prioritized.
methodology: Survey and position paper reviewing human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning literature, identifying requirements, challenges, and opportunities.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how degradation or homogenization of web-scraped training data affects the quality of human feedback signals used in HITL RL.
